title: Why exercise alone does not heal trauma but movement can
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

I ran three miles and still felt like dying inside 

 I did everything right. I woke up at 5:30 AM. I laced up my sneakers. I ran three miles in the cold...
htmlContent:
I ran three miles and still felt like dying inside

I did everything right. I woke up at 5:30 AM. I laced up my sneakers. I ran three miles in the cold morning air. My heart pounded. My lungs burned. I felt alive. Then I went home, showered, and stood in the kitchen feeling completely empty. The same old dread crept back into my chest. The same tightness in my throat. The same sense that something was terribly wrong, even though I had just done the thing everyone says will fix you.

Exercise. It's supposed to be the cure, right? Just move your body. Sweat it out. Get those endorphins flowing. I tried that for years. I ran marathons. I lifted weights. I did hot yoga until I thought I might pass out. And none of it healed the trauma living inside my nervous system. Here's the thing: exercise alone cannot heal trauma. But movement can. There's a difference, and it matters more than you might think.

I'm not saying exercise is bad. It's not. Movement is essential for health. But when we confuse exercise with trauma recovery, we set ourselves up for failure. We end up feeling broken because we did the thing we were supposed to do and still felt terrible. That's not your fault. It's a misunderstanding of how trauma lives in the body.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote the book on trauma and the body, spent decades studying this. He found that trauma isn't stored in your thoughts or your memories alone. It lives in your physical body. In your muscles. In your fascia. In your nervous system. Van der Kolk says that traditional exercise often bypasses the very systems that need attention. You can run a marathon and still have a nervous system that's stuck in survival mode. You can have six-pack abs and still have a body that doesn't feel safe.

The problem with pushing through

Most exercise culture is built on a simple premise: push harder. Go faster. Do more. No pain, no gain. That works fine for building muscle or improving cardiovascular fitness. But for a traumatized nervous system, pushing through is exactly the wrong approach. Stay with me here, because this gets at something important.

When you have trauma, your nervous system is already in a state of high alert. It's already pushing. It's already fighting or fleeing or freezing. Adding more intensity, more pressure, more "push through it" messaging can actually reinforce the trauma response. You're teaching your body that it needs to override its own signals. That discomfort doesn't matter. That your boundaries don't count.

I learned this the hard way. I used to do high-intensity interval training until I vomited. I thought that meant I was healing. I thought the more I punished my body, the more I would overcome my past. But I was just recreating the same pattern of ignoring my body's warnings. The same pattern that got me into trouble in the first place.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about this directly. He says trauma gets stuck in the body when the natural fight-or-flight response is interrupted or overwhelmed. The energy gets trapped. It doesn't discharge. And if you just keep pushing through without addressing that trapped energy, you're just building a bigger charge without releasing it. Think about it like a pressure cooker. You can keep adding heat, but if you never let the steam out, eventually something breaks.

What movement does that exercise doesn't

So what's the difference? Why does movement work when exercise fails? It comes down to one thing: your nervous system's perception of safety. Exercise, as we usually do it, is goal-oriented. It's about performance, achievement, results. Movement, on the other hand, is about sensation, presence, and connection to the body. It's not about how many reps you did or how fast you ran. It's about how you felt while doing it.

When I finally stopped trying to exercise my trauma away and started moving with intention, everything shifted. I slowed down. I stopped tracking my heart rate. I stopped caring about calories burned. I started paying attention to what my body actually needed. Some days that meant gentle stretching on the floor. Some days it meant walking slowly through the woods. Some days it meant shaking my arms and legs to release tension. Some days it meant lying on the ground and breathing.

That's not exercise. That's movement. And it's what your nervous system actually craves.

Deb Dana, a clinician who specializes in polyvagal theory, talks about the importance of "vagal tone." Your vagus nerve is the main nerve of your parasympathetic nervous system. It's what helps you calm down after stress. It's what allows you to feel safe and connected. And you can improve your vagal tone through specific kinds of movement. Not through pounding your body into submission, but through gentle, rhythmic, social movements that signal safety to your brain.

Think about what happens when you rock a baby. Or when you sway back and forth to music. Or when you walk with a friend and talk. Those movements activate the ventral vagal pathway. They tell your body, "We're safe. We're with others. We can relax." That's the opposite of what most exercise does.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Levine walks through how to work with the body's natural healing mechanisms. It's not about pushing harder. It's about listening.

How to actually move for trauma recovery

I'm going to give you some practical things to try. But first, a warning: none of this is about perfection. You will not do it right. That's the point. Trauma recovery through movement is about letting go of "right" and "wrong" and just being with what is. Wild, right? The thing that's supposed to heal you requires you to stop trying so hard.

Start with your breath. Before any movement, just notice your breath. Don't change it. Just notice. Is it shallow or deep? Fast or slow? Where do you feel it in your body? This alone can start to shift your nervous system. Then, if it feels okay, take a few slow exhales. Make them longer than your inhales. This activates the vagus nerve. It's like pressing a reset button on your nervous system.

Then try gentle shaking. Stand up and just shake your hands. Then your arms. Then your legs. Shake your whole body like a dog coming out of water. This is how animals discharge trauma. They shake it off. We can do the same thing. It might feel silly. That's okay. Do it anyway. Notice what happens in your body after 30 seconds of shaking.

Try walking without a destination. Not for exercise. Not to get your steps in. Just walk. Notice the ground under your feet. Notice the air on your skin. Notice the movement of your hips. This kind of walking is meditative. It's grounding. It tells your body that you are moving through space safely. A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Gabor Mate explains how chronic stress and unexpressed emotion get stored in the body. Movement helps release that.

Try stretching with your eyes closed. When you close your eyes, you turn off your visual system. Your other senses become more acute. You can feel more deeply into your body. You might notice places where you're holding tension that you didn't even know about. Just breathe into those places. Don't force them to relax. Just be with them.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk includes a chapter on yoga and movement therapy. He talks about how yoga helped trauma survivors reconnect with their bodies in a way that traditional exercise never could. It's not about the poses. It's about the internal experience.

What to do when you can't move at all

Here's the thing nobody talks about: sometimes trauma makes movement feel impossible. Sometimes you're so frozen that even the thought of getting up feels overwhelming. That's not laziness. That's your nervous system protecting you. It's a freeze response. And pushing through it with exercise will only make it worse.

If you're in that place, start smaller. Start with just moving your eyes. Look slowly around the room. Notice colors and shapes. Then move your fingers. Then your toes. Then your wrists and ankles. Tiny movements. Micro-movements. They still count. They still send signals to your nervous system that you are alive and present and capable of choice.

You can also try orienting. This is a technique from Somatic Experiencing. Look around your environment and notice things that feel safe. A comfortable chair. A soft blanket. A window with sunlight. Let your eyes rest on those things for a few seconds. This tells your brain that you are in a safe place right now. That the danger is not currently present. It can start to bring you out of freeze.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana writes about how to find your "safe and connected" state. She gives exercises for building vagal tone. It's gentle. It's slow. It's the opposite of pushing through.

I want you to hear this: you don't have to exercise to heal. You don't have to run. You don't have to lift. You don't have to sweat. You just have to move in ways that feel okay. That might be five minutes of stretching. That might be a slow walk around the block. That might be just rocking in a chair. It's all valid. It's all healing. Because it's all sending the same message to your nervous system: I am here. I am safe. I am in charge of my body now.

Healing Journey Resources

  • When the Body Says No (paid link)
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link)
